g.data-narration: Welcome to Apache Tomcat - your gateway to servlet development and Java web applications.
Apache Tomcat is an open-source servlet container implementing Java Servlet and JSP specifications for web development.
g.text: Open-source Java Servlet Container implementing Jakarta EE specifications
Reference implementation for Servlets, JSP, and WebSocket

g.data-narration: Over 60% of Java web applications use Tomcat. It demonstrates servlet lifecycle, deployment, and real-world skills.
Tomcat has six key directories: bin for scripts, conf for config, logs for errors, webapps for deployments, work for temp files.
A WAR file contains WEB-INF with web.xml, classes for servlets, and lib for dependencies. Public resources go outside WEB-INF.
Deploy WAR files by copying to webapps folder, using Tomcat Manager web interface, or directly from your IDE like Eclipse.
web.xml maps URL patterns to servlet classes. The servlet-name connects servlet declaration to its mapping for request routing.
Tomcat Manager at port 8080 lets you start, stop, and deploy applications. Configure users in tomcat-users.xml with manager-gui role.
Debug 404 errors by checking URL mapping, 500 errors by reviewing servlet compilation, and port conflicts in server.xml.
g.text: logs/catalina.out - Check here for all errors!

g.data-narration: Create servlet, compile to WEB-INF/classes, create web.xml, build WAR with jar command, then deploy to webapps folder.
Tomcat controls servlet lifecycle: init() called once, service() handles each request, destroy() cleanup on undeployment.
Key exam topics: WAR structure with WEB-INF/classes, port in server.xml, web.xml servlet mapping, and error code meanings.
You now understand Tomcat for servlet development: directories, WAR structure, deployment methods, web.xml, and debugging.
g.text: ✓ Deploy: Copy to webapps/ or use Manager App
